video_title: Lawfare Daily: Taking Stock of the Ukraine-Russia Talks
gist: Two Ukraine-Russia negotiation experts, Eric Ciaramella (Carnegie Endowment) and Samuel Charap (RAND), dissect why the Trump administration's US-mediated talks stalled after early 2026, arguing the US made two central errors — elevating the Donbas territorial question too early and floating a non-credible Article 5-style security guarantee — while debating more credible deterrence and negotiation-structure alternatives going forward.

themes.theme: Russia's War Optimism and Putin's Opaque Calculus

insights.claim: Ukraine's deep drone strikes inside Russia and 'middle strikes' against the logistics corridor connecting Crimea to Russia have forced the Russian military and economic managers into trade-offs they did not previously have to make, marking a distinct new phase of pressure in the war.
insights.speaker: Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Sustained Ukrainian strike pressure on Russian logistics is a leading indicator to watch for shifts in Russian negotiating posture, though it has not yet visibly changed Kremlin strategy.

insights.claim: Despite mounting battlefield pressure on Russia, there is no evidence Putin has personally concluded he needs to change strategic course, since the war's trajectory ultimately hinges on one man's largely unknowable internal calculation.
insights.speaker: Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Investors and policymakers should discount signals like oligarch essays or partial admissions of problems as insufficient to predict a Russian pivot until top-level decision-making visibly shifts.
insights.quote: he himself is a black box and he may very well not have any idea himself what course he wants to take over the next 6 to 12 months.

insights.claim: Putin has proven willing to subject the Russian population and economy to a much higher level of sustained pain than Western policymakers originally expected, suggesting economic pressure alone has a high threshold before it forces concessions.
insights.speaker: Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Sanctions and economic-pressure-based strategies should be evaluated with a longer time horizon and higher pain tolerance assumption for the Kremlin than initially modeled.
insights.quote: he's been willing to uh sustain a much higher level of pain than Western policy makers, I think, expected.

insights.claim: Even if Putin privately recognizes Russia's battlefield and economic problems, he has strong incentives not to show diminished optimism publicly given upcoming Russian legislative elections and his reportedly sinking domestic popularity.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Public Kremlin bravado should not be read as a reliable signal of Putin's actual private assessment of the war's trajectory.
insights.quote: He's very little incentive to let that on in public, right?

insights.claim: Because Russia currently lacks any credible alternative to continued fighting, it is likely to keep prosecuting the war even if its underlying battlefield optimism has diminished, a pattern consistent with historical war-termination dynamics.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: A durable ceasefire is unlikely until negotiators construct a genuinely credible off-ramp for Russia, not just battlefield pressure alone.
insights.quote: I don't think there is really a credible alternative that Putin is looking at for even to explore a process that could be there to explore what a credible alternative to continued fighting might look like.

themes.theme: Negotiation Structure and the US as Mediator

insights.claim: A non-neutral US serving as mediator between Ukraine and Russia can theoretically work, drawing on the historical precedent of US mediation of the Yom Kippur War aftermath and Egypt-Israel/Syria-Israel agreements despite the US openly backing Israel militarily at the time.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Arguments that the US is disqualified from mediating because it backs Ukraine are historically weak; the real requirement is that both parties trust the mediator's objectives, not its neutrality.

insights.claim: The two lead US negotiators on the Ukraine-Russia talks have no dedicated negotiating team of their own and are simultaneously responsible for separate negotiations with Iran, a structural resourcing gap Eric Ciaramella views as a core reason the talks have underperformed.
insights.speaker: Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Watch whether the US expands or professionalizes its negotiating team as a leading indicator of whether the next round of talks will be taken more seriously.

insights.claim: Jared Kushner's approach to the talks reflects a freewheeling, business-style negotiating philosophy carried over from his Abraham Accords experience, reinforced by his belief that traditional conflict-diplomacy structures (UN involvement, career experts) have failed.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Expect continued avoidance of traditional multilateral diplomatic structures and expert staffing in future rounds unless this philosophy shifts.

insights.claim: The negotiations have proceeded episodically, with meetings roughly every six weeks rather than continuously, which Samuel Charap argues is inadequate for resolving complex, multi-issue international disputes that historically require regular, sustained engagement to build trade space across issues.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: A shift to continuous, confidential, party-owned negotiation sessions would be a concrete signal that the process is being taken more seriously and could produce a settlement faster.
insights.quote: you need to actually get people in a room like regularly, not every few months

insights.claim: Negotiations have been conducted in disconnected silos (a separate US-Ukraine security track, a Europe-Ukraine track, and a US-Russia track), which prevents cross-issue trade-offs and leaves open the critical unknown of whether Russia has actually signed off on the US-Ukraine security guarantee that Zelensky has described as nearly fully agreed.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Any 'nearly finalized' US-Ukraine agreement should be treated skeptically as a signal of overall deal progress until Russian buy-in to the broader package is confirmed.
insights.quote: we have no idea if the Russians have signed off on this US-Ukraine security guarantee that apparently is 100% or 90% or 95% agreed

themes.theme: How the Donbas Became the Central Sticking Point

insights.claim: The territorial question became the central issue in the talks largely because Steve Witkoff put it on the table early, in the Moscow meeting preceding the Anchorage summit, rather than because it was objectively Russia's top priority from the start.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: The prominence of the territorial issue in current talks is partly an artifact of US negotiating sequencing, not a fixed Russian redline, meaning renewed sequencing could de-emphasize it again.

insights.claim: Both analysts agree that elevating the Donbas territorial question to the center of negotiations at Anchorage was a major US negotiating error, because Russia came away believing the territory had effectively already been conceded and had incentive to 'pocket' that perceived concession and move the goalposts rather than compromise further.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Future negotiators should avoid granting apparent early concessions on high-value issues without securing reciprocal, binding commitments in the same exchange.
insights.quote: a combination of Whitcoff's blunder and Russian craftiness uh about trying to get an early major concession and then pocket it and move the goalposts

insights.claim: The two guests disagree on how locked-in Putin is to fully seizing Donbas: Samuel Charap argues Putin's repeated public commitments since the 2022 annexation announcement have boxed him into treating full territorial capture as near-mandatory, while Eric Ciaramella believes Putin remains firmly enough in control of Russia's information environment to redefine 'victory' and retreat from that framing whenever it suits him.
insights.speaker: Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Do not assume a Russian territorial climbdown is politically impossible for Putin; watch for propaganda narrative shifts as the real tell rather than treating stated Donbas demands as immovable.
insights.quote: there's a lot of bravado in public up until the moment that they make a pivot, and then they make you think that the pivot was the plan all along.

insights.claim: Russian pro-war commentators and 'patriotic camp' Telegram accounts are already circulating narratives that Russia's original 'special military operation' objectives were achieved and that a long-term pause or armistice would itself constitute a Russian victory, providing Putin a ready-made off-ramp narrative independent of actual territorial gains.
insights.speaker: Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: A Russian ceasefire acceptance could arrive with little additional territorial concession, since domestic narrative management gives Putin flexibility to declare victory on existing terms.

insights.claim: A proposed negotiating tactic is to shelve the zero-sum territorial question first, work through other political and security issues, and then retest whether the territorial dispute becomes easier to resolve once mutual understandings exist elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Negotiators (and observers) should watch for a sequencing shift that defers territory to gauge whether this untested approach gains traction in the next round.

themes.theme: The Credibility Problem With an Article 5-Style Guarantee

insights.claim: The Trump administration's proposed Article 5-like bilateral US-Ukraine security guarantee, which Zelensky says is 100% agreed in text and under which the US would determine future measures including armed force if Russia attacks again, is fundamentally not credible because it promises future US direct military intervention that the US is currently unwilling to undertake.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: A headline 'security guarantee' announcement should not be treated as materially reducing invasion risk unless backed by demonstrated present-day commitments, not just paper promises of future action.
insights.quote: the the basic problem with it is it's not credible.

insights.claim: If an Article 5-style US-Ukraine guarantee were tested by Russia and the US failed to act, it would call into question all of the US's other global security commitments and could embroil the US in a war with Russia that risks escalation to the nuclear level.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: The downside tail risk of an untested, overextended security guarantee is not just a failed Ukraine policy but a credibility shock to the broader US alliance system.

insights.claim: Genuine NATO Article 5 deterrence rests on decades of joint political and military commitments, force stationing, and contingency planning; a standalone Ukraine guarantee would lack all of that supporting infrastructure, making it, in Eric Ciaramella's words, 'a check written without the money in the account.'
insights.speaker: Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Markets and policymakers should not price a Ukraine security guarantee as equivalent to actual NATO membership-level deterrence.
insights.quote: a check that would be written without the money in the account

insights.claim: Rather than a broad Article 5-style pledge, Eric Ciaramella argues a more credible approach would codify already-demonstrated forms of US support (long-term Ukrainian military capacity building, allied-resourced weapons stockpiles, and defense production commitments) under a dedicated governance mechanism resembling a 'North Atlantic Council' for Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Watch for institutional proposals (a Ukraine-specific governance body, pre-positioned stockpiles) as a more credible and durable deterrence signal than headline guarantee language.

insights.claim: The two analysts disagree on whether a US-Ukraine security guarantee needs Russian sign-off to be effective: Samuel Charap sees Russian buy-in as important (unlike the 2022 Istanbul framework where Russia would have been a formal party), while Eric Ciaramella argues the guarantee can be built purely through US-Ukraine channels and then simply presented to Russia as an established fact on the ground.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: The structural design choice of whether to negotiate a guarantee with or without Russia at the table will shape whether it functions as a genuine deterrent or an unverified unilateral pledge.

insights.claim: A bipartisan provision in this year's Senate Intelligence Authorization Act, backed by Senator Cotton and Senator Bennet, lays out a three-phase framework for US intelligence support to Ukraine: continued real-time targeting intelligence until a ceasefire, a transition to longer-term capacity-building support during an armistice, and reversion to full real-time targeting support if Russia re-invades.
insights.speaker: Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: This legislative provision is a concrete, trackable proxy for how credible future US deterrence commitments to Ukraine will be structured, and is worth monitoring as it moves through Congress.
insights.quote: To me, that is eminently reasonable, and it does codify a future commitment that I think is credible in this case.

insights.claim: Any future 'snapback' of US support to Ukraine would start from a much higher baseline than February 2022, when the US had only provided Javelins and Stingers, since Ukraine has since received F-16s, tanks, HIMARS, and ATACMS — meaning pre-positioned stockpiles and resourced defense production plans could make the threat of renewed support more credible to Russia.
insights.speaker: Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Assess future deterrence proposals against this higher capability baseline rather than the pre-invasion 2022 starting point, since the credible 'threat' of resumed aid is now materially larger.

insights.claim: An effective snapback mechanism should be 'total,' conditioning all Russian benefits from any agreement — including political and military restraint commitments, not just resumed Western aid to Ukraine — on continued Russian ceasefire compliance.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Deal terms should be evaluated for whether Russian-side benefits (not just Ukrainian-side aid pauses) are also reversible on violation, which is a stronger deterrent design than one-sided snapback.

insights.claim: No single deterrence mechanism (Article 5-style guarantees, intelligence snapback, stockpiles) should be treated as a silver bullet against a determined Russia; a capable, well-armed Ukrainian military remains the primary deterrent, with other measures serving as supplements.
insights.speaker: Samuel Charap, RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Long-term investment in Ukraine's own military capacity should be weighted as the core deterrence variable, with security guarantees and snapback clauses as secondary reinforcement rather than the primary safeguard.
insights.quote: we're not talking about silver bullets of deterrence that if Russia is determined to go to war, these are not going to like be insurmountable roadblocks.
